mcqs.question: Which multithreading model maps many user-level threads to a single kernel thread?
mcqs.explanation: The Many-to-One model maps multiple user threads to a single kernel thread. Thread management is handled in user space by a thread library.

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of the Many-to-One threading model?
mcqs.explanation: The main disadvantage is that if one thread makes a blocking system call, the entire process blocks, and it cannot take advantage of multiprocessor systems since all threads run on a single processor.

mcqs.question: Which threading model provides true concurrency and can leverage multiple processors effectively?
mcqs.explanation: The One-to-One model maps each user thread to a separate kernel thread, allowing the operating system to schedule threads on different processors, enabling true concurrency.

mcqs.question: In a multithreaded process, what happens when a thread calls fork() in most UNIX systems?

options.text: All threads are duplicated in the new process
Only the calling thread is duplicated in the new process
The fork() call fails with an error

mcqs.explanation: In most UNIX systems, when a thread calls fork(), only the calling thread is duplicated in the new process. This behavior prevents issues that might occur if all threads were duplicated.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is an advantage of kernel-level threads over user-level threads?

options.text: Faster thread creation and context switching
Better portability across different operating systems
Ability to run threads on multiple processors simultaneously

mcqs.explanation: Kernel-level threads can be scheduled by the OS to run on different processors simultaneously, enabling true parallelism on multiprocessor systems, which is not possible with user-level threads.
